title: First Chords
description: Learn the four most essential ukulele chords: C, Am, F, and G.

steps.title: Welcome to Ukulele!
steps.content: These four chords — C, Am, F, and G — are the foundation of hundreds of songs. Master these, and you'll be playing real music in no time.

steps.title: C Major
steps.content: The simplest chord on ukulele! Place your ring finger on the 3rd fret of the A string (bottom string). All other strings are open.
steps.tips: Press firmly right behind the fret wire, not on top of it.

steps.title: A Minor
steps.content: Place your index finger on the 2nd fret of the G string (top string). Everything else is open.
steps.tips: This chord has a beautiful, melancholy sound. Notice how it's just one finger!

steps.title: F Major
steps.content: Place your index finger on the 1st fret of the E string, and your middle finger on the 2nd fret of the G string.
steps.tips: Keep your fingers arched so they don't accidentally mute the open strings.

steps.title: G Major
steps.content: This is the trickiest of the four. Index finger on 2nd fret of C string, ring finger on 3rd fret of E string, middle finger on 2nd fret of A string.
steps.tips: Take your time with this one. It gets easier with practice!

steps.title: Chord Switching: C to Am
steps.content: Practice switching between C and Am. Try to switch smoothly without pausing.

steps.title: Chord Switching: F to G
steps.content: Now try F to G. This is a bigger jump.

steps.title: The Four-Chord Loop
steps.content: Put it all together! Play C, Am, F, G in a loop.

title: Strumming Patterns
description: Learn essential strumming patterns to bring your chords to life.

steps.title: Why Strumming Matters
steps.content: Chords are the vocabulary, but strumming is the rhythm. A simple chord with a great strum pattern sounds better than a complex chord strummed badly.

steps.title: Basic Down Strum
steps.content: Strum down on every beat. Count 1-2-3-4 and strum down each time. Keep your wrist loose.

steps.title: Down-Up Pattern
steps.content: Alternate: down on the beat, up on the 'and'. Count: 1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and.

steps.title: Island Strum
steps.content: The classic ukulele pattern! Down, Down-Up, Up-Down-Up. This is the pattern behind Hawaiian music and pop songs.

steps.title: Calypso Strum
steps.content: A syncopated pattern with Caribbean feel.

steps.title: Practice with C and F
steps.content: Apply the Island Strum to a C-F chord change. Play 4 bars of C, then 4 bars of F.

title: Fingerpicking Basics
description: Learn fingerpicking technique for a gentler, more melodic sound.

steps.title: Fingerpicking Introduction
steps.content: Fingerpicking creates a beautiful, harp-like sound. Your thumb handles the G and C strings, while your index and middle fingers handle E and A.

steps.title: Hand Position
steps.content: Rest your thumb on the G string. Let your index finger hover over the E string, and your middle finger over the A string. Keep relaxed.

steps.title: Simple Arpeggio
steps.content: Play strings one at a time: G (thumb) then C (thumb) then E (index) then A (middle). Hold a C chord shape.

steps.title: Reverse Arpeggio
steps.content: Now reverse: A (middle) then E (index) then C (thumb) then G (thumb). Keep C chord held.

steps.title: Travis Picking Pattern
steps.content: Thumb alternates G and C strings while fingers pick E and A.

title: Barre Chords
description: Master the barre chord technique to unlock every chord on the fretboard.

steps.title: What Are Barre Chords?
steps.content: A barre chord uses one finger to press multiple strings across a single fret. This creates a moveable shape — slide it up and down to play any chord.

steps.title: Building Finger Strength
steps.content: Barre chords require finger strength. Place your index finger flat across all 4 strings at fret 1. Press firmly and strum — every string should ring clearly.

steps.title: Bb Major (Your First Barre)
steps.content: Barre all strings at fret 1 with your index finger, then add: middle on fret 2 C string, ring on fret 3 E string, pinky on fret 3 A string.
steps.tips: Roll your index finger slightly to the side for better pressure.

steps.title: B Major
steps.content: Move the Bb shape up one fret. Barre at fret 2, same shape above.
steps.tips: Same pattern — every barre shape slides up to make a new chord!

steps.title: Barre Chord Movement
steps.content: Move a barre chord shape from fret 1 (Bb) to fret 3 (C) to fret 5 (D) and back.

title: Music Theory for Uke
description: Understand keys, scales, and why certain chords sound good together.

steps.title: Why Learn Theory?
steps.content: Music theory isn't rules — it's patterns. Once you see them, you can figure out chords by ear, write progressions, and communicate with other musicians.

steps.title: The Major Scale
steps.content: Every key is built from whole and half steps (W-W-H-W-W-W-H). In C: C-D-E-F-G-A-B. No sharps or flats.

steps.title: Chords From the Scale
steps.content: Each note gets a chord: C(I), Dm(ii), Em(iii), F(IV), G(V), Am(vi), Bdim(vii). Major chords are uppercase Roman, minor lowercase.

steps.title: The Magic Four Chords
steps.content: I-V-vi-IV is the most common pop progression. In C: C-G-Am-F. In G: G-D-Em-C.

steps.title: I-V-vi-IV in C
steps.content: Play C, G, Am, F — the famous four-chord progression.

steps.title: I-V-vi-IV in G
steps.content: Same progression in G: G, D, Em, C. Notice how it feels the same?

steps.title: The Circle of Fifths
steps.content: Keys relate by fifths: C-G-D-A-E-B-F#-C#-Ab-Eb-Bb-F-C. Adjacent keys share most chords.
